narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the unique challenges and strengths you bring from your background. The admissions model evaluated your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 29, Year 1 school decile of 1st (well below the typical 6th decile), Year 3 decile of 2nd (also lower than the average 6th), middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. It predicted a 60% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold that led to your application's rejection—schools use this to prioritize candidates likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factors pushing toward this failure prediction were your lower Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles compared to peers, as these early and later academic rankings stood out as stronger signals of potential bar struggles than average for students in the dataset. Your undergraduate GPA played a smaller role but still nudged slightly toward the failure prediction relative to typical applicants. On a more positive note, your full-time enrollment plan and LSAT score helped pull the prediction toward success, aligning with patterns seen in students who pass, while your middle-income background offered a modest favorable influence. Overall, your profile compared to typical students—who often have higher deciles around the 6th, LSATs near 37, and GPAs around 3.2—tipped the scales due to those early academic standings being the top drivers. This doesn't define your potential; many overcome similar starts through targeted preparation. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergrad record with post-bac courses or relevant experience, and seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a standout narrative. Reapply after addressing these, perhaps to schools with holistic reviews that value your resilience. We're rooting for you—your journey is just beginning.
